schools.summary: Gill: having dispatched the affair of the incestuous person, Paul proceeds to another evil — that when differences arose about their worldly concerns, they would go to law before the heathen courts; Paul rebuking the Corinthians for daring to take their disputes before the unjust (the heathen, unbelieving magistrates) rather than before the saints — the scandal of believers airing their quarrels in pagan courts (the 'dare' marking the boldness/audacity of the wrong, exposing the church's disputes to the world, seeking justice from the unrighteous), the better way of settling matters among themselves before the saints, the impropriety (the saints, who shall judge the world, v.2, going to the unjust for judgment).

themes: Dare any of you... go to law before the unjust
not before the saints
the scandal of believers in pagan courts
settling among the saints

schools.summary: Gill: this was a known notion (the righteous should judge the world); Barnes: the object to show that Christians were qualified to determine such matters; Paul appealing to the truth that the saints shall judge the world (sharing in Christ's judgment, approving his sentence, sitting with him, Matthew 19:28; Revelation 20:4, or condemning the world by their faith and lives, as the men of Nineveh, Matthew 12:41) — arguing that those who will judge the world are surely competent to judge the smallest earthly matters (the greater including the less, the dignity of the saints making the argument, such exalted ones should not go to the heathen for trifles).

debates.question: How will 'the saints judge the world'?

sides.position: By sharing in Christ's judgment — sitting with him, approving his sentence (Matthew 19:28; Revelation 20:4), and/or condemning the world by their faith and example

themes: the saints shall judge the world
are ye unworthy to judge the smallest matters?
the dignity of the saints
the saints' competence to judge

schools.summary: Gill: this means not the ministers of the gospel, nor the good angels, but the evil angels (the devil and his angels), whom the saints shall judge; Paul adding that the saints shall judge angels (sharing in the condemnation of the fallen angels, approving and concurring in their judgment at the last day) — how much more the things of this life (the petty earthly affairs) — the further dignity of the saints (an exalted destiny, the judging of angels a stronger argument than the judging of the world, v.2, for angels are higher than men), making them surely competent for the things of this life (the trifles compared to the judging of angels).

themes: we shall judge angels
how much more things that pertain to this life?
the saints' exalted destiny
competent for earthly matters

schools.summary: Gill: these are 'pecuniary judgments' (about the common affairs of life), not capital ones (which were in the Roman magistrate's power); such matters should be judged within the church; Paul directing that, in earthly disputes, they set even the least esteemed in the church to judge (rather than go to the heathen) — the principle that even the lowliest believer is more fit to judge the church's earthly matters than the pagan courts (for he has the Spirit and the wisdom of God), the disputes to be settled within the body by its own members, however humble (the matters kept in the church, not aired before the heathen).

themes: judgments of things pertaining to this life
set them to judge who are least esteemed in the church
even the lowliest believer more fit than the heathen
the matters kept in the church

schools.summary: Gill: Paul speaks to their shame — not that they did set such persons to judge, but that they did not, and instead went to law before the unjust; Paul speaking to their shame, asking (ironically) whether there was not one wise man among them able to judge between the brethren — the rebuke of their folly (that a church so boastful of its wisdom, gifts, and eloquence, 1 Corinthians 1-4, could not find one wise enough to arbitrate a dispute, but must run to the heathen), the shame of seeking pagan judgment while professing wisdom (the pretence deflated, the disputes aired before the world).

themes: I speak to your shame
is it so, that there is not a wise man among you?
could no one judge between brethren?
the folly of seeking pagan judgment

schools.summary: Gill: the relation is spiritual (members called brethren, of the same family under Christ); it was a wicked and shameful thing that persons in such a relation should go to law with one another at all; Paul exposing the aggravated evil — brother going to law with brother, and that before unbelievers — the double wrong (that brethren of one spiritual family should sue one another at all, the breach of brotherly love, and that they should do so before the unbelieving heathen, the scandal of airing the family's quarrels before the world, dishonouring Christ and reproaching the gospel).

themes: brother goeth to law with brother
before the unbelievers
the double wrong
the scandal of airing quarrels before the world

schools.summary: Gill: there was 'utterly a fault' — a want of brotherly love (else no lawsuit), of wisdom (else arbiters chosen), and of care in their leaders; Paul declaring that the very having of lawsuits is a fault, and asking why they did not rather take wrong and suffer themselves to be defrauded — the higher way of love (better to suffer wrong, forgo one's rights, endure the injury, than to sue a brother), the spirit of Christ that yields rather than contends (who, when reviled, reviled not again, 1 Peter 2:23; Matthew 5:39-40), the contrast of the yielding spirit and the contending spirit.

themes: there is utterly a fault among you
why do ye not rather take wrong?
rather suffer yourselves to be defrauded
the higher way of suffering wrong

schools.summary: Barnes: instead of enduring wrong patiently and cheerfully, they were themselves guilty of injustice and fraud — and that toward their fellow Christians, as if they had injured those of their own family; Paul charging that, far from taking wrong (v.7), they did wrong and defrauded — and that their own brethren — the aggravated evil (not only failing to suffer wrong but actively wronging and defrauding others, and those their brethren), the reversal of the better way, the injustice done to the family of God (those who should be loved, helped, and dealt with justly), the depth of the Corinthian corruption.

themes: ye do wrong, and defraud
and that your brethren
the reversal of the better way
injustice to the family of God

schools.summary: Gill: there is no entrance into the world of bliss without a righteousness — and that better than a sinful creature can work, even the righteousness of Christ; Paul warning that the unrighteous shall not inherit the kingdom of God, and charging them 'be not deceived,' listing the excluding sins (fornicators, idolaters, adulterers, the effeminate and abusers of themselves with mankind — the sexually immoral, including homosexual practice) — the solemn warning that those who live in such sins (impenitently, as their settled course) have no inheritance in the kingdom, the warning against the deception (the false presumption that one may live in sin and yet be saved), the need of Christ's righteousness and the holy life.

themes: the unrighteous shall not inherit the kingdom of God
be not deceived
the list of excluding sins
the exclusion of those who live in sin

schools.summary: Barnes: it is remarkable that the apostle always ranks the covetous with the most abandoned classes of men; Paul continuing the list of excluding sins — thieves, covetous, drunkards, revilers, extortioners — who shall not inherit the kingdom of God — the comprehensive exclusion (extending beyond the sensual sins of v.9 to the sins of greed, covetous/thieves/extortioners, intemperance, drunkards, and the tongue, revilers), the covetous ranked with the grossest sinners (often excused as respectable but condemned with thieves and drunkards, the warning to the greedy), the warning reaching all.

themes: nor thieves, nor covetous, nor drunkards
shall not inherit the kingdom of God
the covetous ranked with the grossest
the comprehensive exclusion

schools.summary: Gill: 'such were some of you' — not all, nor each guilty of all, but such sins had been committed by one or another (the Corinthians a people much given to uncleanness and luxury, yet God having chosen vessels of salvation among them); Paul declaring that such were some of them, but now they are washed (cleansed from the guilt and filth of sin), sanctified (made holy, set apart, renewed), and justified (declared righteous, Christ's righteousness imputed) — the triumph of grace over the worst sins (the change from the vice-list, vv.9-10, to the saints), wrought 'in the name of the Lord Jesus' (by his merit and work) 'and by the Spirit of our God' (the Trinitarian work), the encouragement that grace can transform the worst sinner.

themes: such were some of you
but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified, but ye are justified
in the name of the Lord Jesus, and by the Spirit
the triumph of grace over the worst sins

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul here glances at the gluttons — speaking not of things forbidden (which are not 'lawful') but of things indifferent; 'it is lawful to eat and drink, but not with excess'; Paul stating that, though all things (indifferent) are lawful for him, not all are expedient (profitable, helpful, fitting), and he will not be brought under the power of any — the limits of Christian liberty (liberty in things indifferent bounded by expediency, is it profitable and helpful, and by mastery, does it enslave, an indifferent thing lawful in itself may enslave if it masters), the wise use of liberty, the two checks.

themes: all things are lawful... but all things are not expedient
I will not be brought under the power of any
Christian liberty and its limits
expediency and mastery

schools.summary: Gill: meats are appointed to satisfy the appetite, and the belly formed to receive them, but both are temporary, to be destroyed; Paul distinguishing meats and the belly (transient, indifferent, to be destroyed) from the body, which is not for fornication but for the Lord — and the Lord for the body — the principle that, while food and the appetite are temporary and indifferent, the body itself is not a thing indifferent to be used for fornication but belongs to the Lord (consecrated to him, a member of Christ, v.15), and the Lord for the body (caring for it, redeeming it, to raise it, v.14); the answer to the libertine argument (that fornication, like eating, was an indifferent bodily appetite — the false analogy broken).

debates.question: Is fornication an indifferent bodily appetite like eating?

sides.position: No — meats and the belly are transient and indifferent, but the body is not for fornication but for the Lord (consecrated to Christ, a member of his body); the libertine analogy is false

themes: meats for the belly, and the belly for meats
the body is not for fornication, but for the Lord
the Lord for the body
the body consecrated to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: God the Father raised up the Lord Jesus from the dead (not excluding the Son, who was concerned in his own resurrection); Barnes: this is the argument that, as God raised Christ, he will raise us; Paul declaring that God raised up the Lord and will also raise up believers by his power — the resurrection of the body the ground for honouring it (since the body is destined to be raised, not destroyed like the belly, v.13, but glorified like Christ's, Philippians 3:21, it is not for fornication but for the Lord), the body's high destiny in the resurrection the argument for its holiness, the contrast with the belly (the destroyed and the raised).

themes: God hath both raised up the Lord
will also raise up us by his own power
the resurrection of the body
the ground for honouring the body
